138	
139	## 4. Sieve Methods and Prime Distribution
140	
141	**Maynard, J.; Tao, T.** Bounded gaps between primes. 2013-2014.
142	Infinitely many prime pairs with gap <= 246. Sieve-based (weighted Selberg sieve).
143	
144	**Craig, W.; Ono, K.; van Ittersum, J.-W.** "Integer partitions detect the primes." PNAS, September 2024. arXiv:2405.06451. [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/2405.06451) [PNAS](https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.2409417121)
145	Primes are solutions to Diophantine equations in MacMahon partition functions. Infinitely many new prime characterizations. Structural, not computational.
146	
147	**Lichtman, J. D.** "A modified linear sieve." Algebra & Number Theory 19(1), 2025. arXiv:2109.02851. [ANT](https://msp.org/ant/2025/19-1/ant-v19-n1-p01-p.pdf)
148	Equidistributes primes in arithmetic progressions to moduli up to x^{10/17}, surpassing the x^{7/12} barrier. Largest improvement to twin prime upper bound since 1986.
149	
150	**Pascadi, A.** "Primes and smooth numbers to x^{5/8}." arXiv:2505.00653, May 2025. [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.00653)
151	Equidistribution to moduli x^{5/8} without Selberg eigenvalue conjecture. Uses triply-well-factorable weights.
152	
153	**Bouras, M.** "A new primes-generating sequence." arXiv:2509.09745, 2025. [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/2509.09745)
154	GCD-based recurrence producing only 1s and primes. Improvement over Rowland but still incomplete.
155	
156	**Tao, T.; Teravainen, J.** "Quantitative Correlations on Prime Factors of Consecutive Integers." arXiv:2512.01739, December 2025. [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.01739)
157	Resolves multiple Erdos conjectures: omega(n+k) <= k infinitely often, irrationality of sum omega(n)/2^n, asymptotic for consecutive integers with equal prime divisor counts.
158	
159	**Volfson, V.** "Generalization of Hardy-Littlewood Conjecture to Almost-Prime Tuples." arXiv:2603.13416, March 2026. [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.13416)
160	Extends Hardy-Littlewood k-tuple conjecture to almost-primes with Selberg constant correction factors.
161	
162	**Hakobyan, T.** "Almost Prime Numbers." arXiv:2603.00679, February 2026. [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00679)
163	New generalization of primality; shows composite almost-primes must be Carmichael numbers.
164	
165	**Li, R.** "Prime-Producing Sieves and Distribution of alpha*p - beta mod 1." arXiv:2504.13195, April 2025. [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/2504.13195)
166	Infinitely many primes p with ||alpha*p - beta|| < p^{-28/87}. Sharpens Jia (2000) using minimal Type-II information.
167	
168	**Gensel, B.** "The Prime Number Formula of Gandhi." arXiv:1910.08362, 2019.
169	Exposition and simplification of Gandhi's primorial formula.
170	
171	**Jakimczuk, R.** "Generalizations of the Gandhi Formula." 2024.
172	Extensions of the Gandhi primorial recurrence.
173	
174	---
175	
176	## 5. Analytic Number Theory and Zeta Function
177	
178	**Cloitre, B.** "An effective analytic recurrence for prime numbers." arXiv:2508.02690, 2025. [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/2508.02690)
179	First proven effective analytic recurrence: p(n+1) via zeta evaluation and Euler product over prior primes. Exact for all n but requires extreme-precision arithmetic.
180	
181	**Saito, K.** "Mills' constant is irrational." arXiv:2404.19461, April 2024; Mathematika 71(3), 2025. [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/2404.19461)
182	Resolved a long-standing open problem. Partial transcendence results obtained.
183	
184	**Visser, M.** "The nth Prime Exponentially." Mathematics (MDPI) 13(11), 1844, 2025. [MDPI](https://www.mdpi.com/2227-7390/13/11/1844)
185	Exponentially tight explicit bounds on p(n) location. Useful for narrowing binary search.
186	
187	**Visser, M.** "Effective exponential bounds on the prime gaps." International Mathematical Forum, 2025. [PDF](https://www.m-hikari.com/imf/imf-2025/1-4-2025/p/visserIMF1-4-2025.pdf)
188	Explicit bounds on g(n) = p(n+1) - p(n) with exponential factors and proven validity ranges.
189	
190	**Visser, M.** "Behaviour of the sequence theta_n = theta(p_n)." arXiv:2507.14410, July 2025.
191	Analysis of Chebyshev's theta function at primes.
192	
193	**Explicit formula for zeta zeros via Hermite polynomials.** arXiv:2312.00108; Ramanujan Journal, 2025. [Springer](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11139-025-01297-y)
194	Claims primes determine zero distribution and zeros can be computed without using zeta directly. Could simplify analytic pi(x) pipeline.
195	
196	**Connes, A.** "The Riemann Hypothesis: Past, Present and a Letter Through Time." arXiv:2602.04022, February 2026. [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04022)
197	42-page survey. Novel: approximates first 50 zeta zeros from primes < 13 to 10^{-55} accuracy using Weil quadratic form. Does not provide computational speedup for pi(x).
198	
199	**Bailleul, A.; Hayani, M.; Untrau, T.** "Wasserstein Approach to Generalized Skewes Numbers." arXiv:2603.20093, March 2026. [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.20093)
200	Unconditionally disproves Fiorilli conjecture. Conditional upper bounds for generalized Skewes numbers using quantitative Kronecker-Weyl theorem in 1-Wasserstein metric.
201	
202	**Vartziotis, D.** "Fourier Series from Additive Prime Factor Functions." arXiv:2602.13342, February 2026. [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.13342)
203	Sparse prime-indexed Fourier series from sopfr(n). Exact decomposition of summatory function B(n) ~ pi^2 x^2/(12 log x). See also companion: arXiv:2602.21270.
204	
205	**Li, Z.** "Deterministic Fractal Set from Prime Number Sequence." arXiv:2603.00658, February 2026. [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.00658)
206	Cantor-like fractal from primes mod 16 with universal Hausdorff dimension 1/4, independent of specific prime residue sequence.
207	
208	---
209	
210	## 6. 2024-2026 Breakthroughs
211	
212	**Guth, L.; Maynard, J.** "New large value estimates for Dirichlet polynomials." arXiv:2405.20552, May 2024; Annals of Mathematics, 2025. [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/2405.20552) [Annals](https://annals.math.princeton.edu/articles/22049)
213	Improved Ingham zero-density bound from 3/5 to 13/25 -- first improvement in 80+ years. PNT in short intervals [x, x+x^{17/30}]. Most significant advance in analytic number theory in 2024.
214	
215	**Gafni, A.; Tao, T.** "Exceptional intervals for the prime number theorem in short intervals." arXiv:2505.24017, May 2025. [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.24017)
216	Explicit quantitative relationship between zero density estimates and exceptional PNT failures. Uses Guth-Maynard estimates.
217	
218	**Le Duc Hieu.** "Arithmetic progressions of primes in short intervals beyond 17/30." arXiv:2509.04883, September 2025. [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/2509.04883)
219	Combines uniform short-interval PNT at theta > 17/30 with Green-Tao transference principle.
220	
221	---
222	
223	## 7. Quantum Computing
224	
225	**Nayak, A.; Wu, F.** Quantum lower bound: Omega(sqrt(x)) queries for functions with prime structure.
226	Proves quantum speedup is at most quadratic over classical for counting-type problems.
227	
228	**Berry-Keating conjecture.** Hypothetical Hamiltonian whose eigenvalues are zeta zeros.
229	Even if it exists, would need ~10^51 eigenvalues for p(10^100). No efficient quantum simulation known.
230	
231	---
232	
233	## 8. Information Theory and ML
234	
235	**Prime Coding Theorem.** arXiv:2308.10817, 2023.
236	Information content of prime sequence exceeds any learnable pattern. Explains why ML cannot discover a prime formula.
237	
238	**Prime classification with sparse encoding.** arXiv:2402.03363, 2024 (WITHDRAWN).
239	ResNet + Transformer for prime/non-prime classification. ~99% recall, never 100%. Withdrawn by authors.
240	
241	**AI in Number Theory: LLMs for Algorithm Generation.** arXiv:2504.19451, April 2025. [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/2504.19451)
242	Qwen2.5-Math-7B achieves >= 0.95 accuracy on algorithmic number theory tasks with optimal hints. LightGBM classifier achieves >= 93.9% accuracy identifying Dirichlet character modulus from L-function zeros.
243	
244	**Google DeepMind.** "AlphaEvolve: A Gemini-Powered Coding Agent for Designing Advanced Algorithms." arXiv:2506.13131, 2025. [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.13131)
245	Evolutionary algorithm discovery: 4x4 matrix multiplication in 48 scalar ops (breaking Strassen), improved solutions on 50+ open problems. Not yet applied to prime computation.
246	
247	---
248	
249	## 9. Summatory Functions and Related
250	
251	**Computation of Totient Summatory Function.** arXiv:2506.07386.
252	Related O(x^{2/3}) method for Euler totient sum. Same barrier as pi(x).

Based on the search results, I found comprehensive information about Lucy_Hedgehog's algorithm for prime counting and its improvements:

## Lucy_Hedgehog DP Algorithm Overview

Lucy_Hedgehog's algorithm, originally posted on Project Euler's Problem 10 forum, was designed to compute the sum of primes under a given number.

## Prime Counting Function with O(x^{2/3}) Complexity

Lucy_Hedgehog's algorithm can be optimized using Fenwick trees to achieve a final runtime of at most O(x^{2/3} (log x)^{1/3}) to compute π(x).

## Lucy DP and Multiplicative Functions

Lucy DP originates from a post by Lucy_Hedgehog in the thread of Project Euler: Problem 10. The algorithm can be applied to summatory multiplicative functions. The Min_25 sieve does an inverse of Lucy DP and computes the sum of f(n) over all integers between 2 and n that were not sieved by primes not greater than x.

## Performance and Extensions

The algorithm can be extended to compute sums of primes and primes in arithmetic progressions. Additionally, both Lucy DP and the black algorithm can be optimized using Fenwick trees, reducing the complexity to O(N^{2/3}).

The basic Lucy_Hedgehog algorithm without optimization uses approximately O(n^{3/4}) time and O(√n) space, making it more efficient than the classical Sieve of Eratosthenes for large values.

Based on the search results, here's what I found regarding Ghaith Hiary's work on computing the Riemann zeta function on the critical line with faster algorithms:

## Main Achievements

The fastest available algorithm for evaluating zeta at a single point has an asymptotic running time of about T^(4/13+o(1)), which is approximately 0.307.

The Riemann zeta function on the critical line can be computed using the Riemann-Siegel formula, Schönhage's method, or Heath-Brown's method, with complexities having exponents 1/2, 3/8 (=0.375), and 1/3 respectively.

## Hiary's Fast Methods

Three new fast methods to compute zeta are presented - one very simple method with complexity exponent 2/5, a second method with complexity exponent 1/3, and a third method with complexity exponent 4/13 (approximately 0.307).

The fastest method has complexity t^(4/13+o(1)), which improves by a noticeable margin on the "complexity bound" of t^(1/3+o(1)).

## Implementation and Applications

One algorithm permits the numerical evaluation of ζ(σ + iT) in T^(1/3+o(1)) time. The main new ingredient in computations of the Riemann zeta function around large values and high zeros is an implementation of Hiary's fast algorithm for numerically evaluating quadratic exponential sums.

The search results provided information from earlier work (primarily 2007-2011 publications), but did not contain specific information about 2024-2025 developments in this area.

I found the paper! Here are the search results for arxiv 2101.08773:

## Paper Summary

The paper presents a new elementary algorithm for computing M(x) = ∑_{n ≤ x} μ(n) (the sum of the Möbius function) that takes time O_ε(x^{3/5} (log x)^{3/5+ε}) and space O(x^{3/10} (log x)^{13/10}).

## Key Contributions

This is the first improvement in the exponent of x for an elementary algorithm since 1985.

Space consumption can be reduced to O(x^{1/5} (log x)^{5/3}) by using earlier work (Helfgott, 2020), at the cost of letting time rise to the order of x^{3/5} (log x).

## Authors

The paper is authored by Harald A. Helfgott and Lola Thompson.

The paper was originally posted on arxiv on January 21, 2021, and was later published in the journal *Research in Number Theory*.

Based on the search results, I found some relevant information related to your query. Here's what I found:

## Black-Box Query Complexity and Polynomial Summation

Research on black-box query complexity has shown that the simple upper bound of 2^n for computing polynomial summation is tight for any field in the black-box model where one has only oracle access to the polynomial.

## Multiplicative Extractors and Black-Box Limitations (2024-2025)

More recently, research has shown that black-box techniques cannot yield extractors with additive error ε=n^(-ω(1)) under certain complexity-theoretic assumptions. A 2025 paper by Ronen Shaltiel on "Multiplicative Extractors for Samplable Distributions" was published in the 40th Computational Complexity Conference (CCC 2025).

## Computational Complexity Results (2024)

Based on the search results, I found relevant information about this topic. The key insight comes from Terry Tao's discussion of why the hyperbola method doesn't work as effectively for the Möbius function and Mertens function compared to other divisor sums.

The inefficiency when using the hyperbola method for the Möbius function compared to the divisor function can be attributed to the signed nature of the Möbius function, which causes some cancellation in the divisor sum expansion that needs to be compensated for with improved estimates.

More specifically, if one wishes to use the standard divisor sum representation with the Möbius function to compute sums, from Dirichlet series methods one ends up with estimates that are useless as lower bounds and recovers only the classical Chebyshev estimate as an upper bound.

The fundamental issue is structural: the divisor function approach works well because it's non-negative, allowing the hyperbola method's geometric lattice-point counting intuition to provide tight bounds. However, the signed nature of the Möbius function causes cancellation in the divisor sum expansion, which prevents straightforward application of the hyperbola method from achieving better than O(x^(1/3)) complexity bounds. This is why different computational techniques, such as sieving methods combined with recursive formulas, are needed to efficiently compute the Mertens function and related sums.

Based on the search results, I can now provide you with a comprehensive answer:

## Helfgott-Thompson Algorithm and the O(x^{3/5}) Bound

A further improvement by Harald Helfgott and Lola Thompson in 2021 improves this to O(x^{3/5}(log x)^{3/5+ε}) for computing the **Mertens function**, which is defined as M(x) = Σ_{n≤x} μ(n), where μ(n) is the Möbius function.

## Does It Apply to π(x)?

**No**, the O(x^{3/5}) bound does **not** directly apply to the prime counting function π(x). The Helfgott-Thompson result is specifically an algorithm for **computing the Mertens function M(x)**, not for computing π(x).

However, there is an indirect relationship: ψ(x)=M(x/2)log 2+M(x/3)log 3+M(x/4)log 4+⋯, where ψ(x) is the Chebyshev function, which is related to π(x). So while the O(x^{3/5}) bound for computing M(x) could potentially be utilized as part of a larger algorithm to estimate π(x) through the Chebyshev function, it is not a direct bound on π(x) itself.

This is the first improvement in the exponent of x for an elementary algorithm since 1985, making it a significant advancement in computational number theory.

The agents are still running. Let me now pursue a different direction: I want to explore the idea of computing pi(x) mod small primes. If we could compute pi(x) mod 2 (the parity of pi(x)) efficiently, that would already be significant.

Actually, let me think about an entirely different angle: **the analytic approach using Perron's formula with a finite number of zeros**.

The key insight from my experiment: the error at x=10^6 with 1000 zeros is ~2.55. This is because pi(x) requires about sqrt(x) = 1000 zeros for the explicit formula to converge. 

Richardson extrapolation brought the error down to 0.49 at that scale, which is promising, but the fundamental question remains: is the error smooth enough that Richardson can reliably improve the approximation across different scales? Looking at my data, the pattern holds at x=10^3 where methods converge quickly, but at x=10^6 Richardson-1 barely works and Richardson-2 fails entirely. The scaling still follows O(sqrt(x)), just with a modest constant factor improvement from extrapolation. Now I'm wondering if there's a completely different approach—what if I could compute pi(x) using only local information about primes near x itself?

For any prime p(n), the inverse Riemann function R^{-1}(n) gives a rough approximation with about 50% accuracy, and the error between the actual prime and this approximation scales as O(sqrt(p(n))/ln(p(n))). The key insight is that maybe I can compute this error using only the local prime distribution near x = R^{-1}(n), rather than needing information about all primes up to x. The local density of primes is roughly 1/ln(x), and deviations from this average come from the Riemann zero sum—but locally, I only need contributions from zeros up to some finite height T. For a short interval around x, the prime count is approximately 2h/ln(x) with error scaling as O(h*sqrt(x)/x).

But wait, to get the exact prime count in a short interval, I still need the full zeta zero sum. Let me pivot to a different strategy entirely: use a probabilistic approach with deterministic verification. I'd estimate p(n) using R^{-1}(n), then recognize that the true prime lies in a bounded interval around this estimate containing roughly O(sqrt(x0)/ln(x0)^2) candidates. To identify which one is actually p(n), I need to know pi(x0) exactly—which brings me back to the same computational bottleneck.

The real issue is that computing pi(x0) requires O(x0^{2/3}) work, and I can't sidestep this by using modular arithmetic or approximations since I need the precise integer value. Let me consider a completely different angle: instead of computing pi(x) and inverting it, what if I directly characterize p(n) using the Prime Number Theorem's explicit error bounds? Under the Riemann Hypothesis, the error in approximating pi(x) by li(x) is bounded by sqrt(x) * ln(x), which means I can relate n to li(p(n)) with a controlled error margin.

This suggests p(n) should be the prime closest to the inverse of the logarithmic integral at n, but finding that prime in a massive interval around 10^102 is itself intractable—I'd either need to sieve with 10^51 primes (impossible to enumerate) or test candidates with probabilistic primality tests, each taking O(log^3(x)) time.

Testing roughly 10^49 candidates this way balloons to 10^55 operations, which beats the naive combinatorial approach but still falls far short of polylog efficiency. I'm realizing this is essentially what the Lagarias-Odlyzko analytic method does: compute pi(x) using the explicit formula with O(sqrt(x)) zeros, then apply local corrections. So I keep hitting the same wall—determining pi(x) exactly always requires O(sqrt(x)) operations or zeros.

The core issue is that prime distribution has O(sqrt(x)/ln(x)) random fluctuations around the mean, encoding O(N) bits of information for primes up to N. Let me pivot entirely and explore whether matrix methods could work differently. The Mertens function M(x) = sum_{n≤x} mu(n) has a recurrence relation that operates over roughly 2*sqrt(x) distinct floor values, which might be expressible as a linear system.

If I frame this as a lower-triangular matrix equation where the matrix entries depend on divisor-like structure, the standard forward substitution approach takes O(x^{2/3}) time. The question is whether fast matrix multiplication algorithms could exploit this structure to do better than the naive O(x) bound that comes from treating it as a generic sqrt(x) × sqrt(x) system.

The matrix A doesn't have simple bandwidth or Toeplitz structure that would let me apply standard fast algorithms. But I'm wondering if I can reformulate the recurrence as a convolution—if the Möbius function recurrence can be expressed that way, FFT-based methods might speed things up. The key observation is that for large k, the values floor(x/k) become dense and cover all integers up to sqrt(x), which might create exploitable structure. I can precompute M(1) through M(sqrt(x)) using a sieve to get the Möbius function values, then calculate prefix sums in O(sqrt(x)) time. The second sum becomes a straightforward O(sqrt(x)) calculation of weighted M values, and for the first sum, each M(floor(x/k)) lookup is constant time since floor(x/k) is already precomputed.

The recursion depth is logarithmic in x, and the total work across all recursive calls is O(x^{2/3}). I'm wondering if there's a way to avoid recursion entirely by computing all M(v) values simultaneously using an algebraic identity. The fundamental identity sum_{d|n} mu(d) = [n=1] rearranges to sum_{d≤x} mu(d) * floor(x/d) = 1, which might unlock a non-recursive approach through generating functions or Dirichlet series.

I'm exploring the connection between M(x) and 1/zeta(s) via Perron's formula, but this path requires knowing the zeros of zeta(s), which circles back to the same computational barrier. Every direction I've tried—whether sieve-based or analytic—hits the same fundamental constraint of needing either O(sqrt(x)) zeta zeros or O(x^{2/3}) operations. Let me check what the background agents have discovered, and I'm also considering whether the functional equation of zeta might offer something I haven't explored yet.

The functional equation relates zeta(s) to zeta(1-s) across the critical line, creating a duality between small and large x behavior, but this symmetry is already implicit in the explicit formula through the conjugate pairs of zeros. I'm now wondering if p-adic methods might bypass the archimedean zeros entirely—the Kubota-Leopoldt p-adic zeta function encodes information about zeta at negative integers, so maybe there's a p-adic approach to computing pi(x) that avoids this bottleneck.

But wait, I'm checking my notes and p-adic/adelic approaches are already marked as closed paths, so that's not going to lead anywhere new. Let me pivot to something different: the additive structure of primes through Goldbach's conjecture and Vinogradov's theorem, which show how primes combine additively rather than multiplicatively.

The question is whether this structure could help computationally—like reconstructing primes up to x from knowing primes up to sqrt(x)—but that doesn't work because additive decompositions only tell us which numbers can be written as prime sums, not which numbers are actually prime.

So stepping back, what would a real breakthrough look like? It would have to be one of three things: a formula for pi(x) that avoids summing over zeta zeros entirely, a way to compute that zeta zero sum in sublinear time relative to sqrt(x), or a proof that polylog computation is fundamentally impossible. The first option seems unlikely since every known formula for pi(x) relies on the zeta zeros in some essential way.

For the second option, the zeta zero sum has O(sqrt(x)) terms that look random and oscillatory, which naively suggests you'd need to read all of them. But here's the thing—the zeta zeros aren't actually independent. They're deterministic and constrained by the functional equation, the Riemann-von Mangoldt formula, and GUE statistics. So the real question becomes whether those constraints allow us to compress the zeta zeros' contribution to the explicit formula, which is exactly what I'm calling the zeta zero compressibility problem.

The spectral flatness of 0.91 tells me the zeros are nearly but not completely random—that 9% departure from true randomness might be exploitable. Though session 10 suggested the incompressible residual is what actually matters for exactness.

I should wait for the background agents to finish, but in the meantime I want to explore the circuit complexity angle more directly. Actually, let me try something concrete: can I compute pi(x) using a meet-in-the-middle approach? The idea is to split the computation into two independent halves that combine afterward. For the combinatorial method where pi(x) = phi(x, a) + a - 1, I can decompose phi recursively and see if each half can be computed in sublinear time.

The baby-step/giant-step approach for the Mertens function looks more promising—splitting the sum into small and large k values with a threshold around x^{1/3}. The first part requires O(x^{1/3}) recursive calls with varying costs, and the second part counts how many k values produce each quotient. The complexity analysis is getting tangled though—I need to work through whether this actually beats the standard algorithm.

Actually, I'm realizing the standard O(x^{2/3}) algorithm IS already using this baby-step/giant-step optimization. The key insight is that by setting the split point at y = x^{1/3}, we compute all small values via a sieve in O(x^{1/3}) time, then handle the larger arguments through recursive calls that collectively take O(x^{2/3}) time. The bottleneck is really in those giant steps—to improve further, I'd need a way to compute M at many points faster than the current approach allows.

I'm wondering if there's a way to batch the computations of M(floor(x/k)) for all k up to x^{1/3}. These values all follow the same recursive structure, so maybe there's shared computation I'm missing. The recursion M(x/k) = 1 - sum_{j=2}^{x/k} M(floor(x/(kj))) shows that each evaluation depends on other values in the same set, which suggests the standard dynamic programming approach—caching M(v) for all distinct values v in the set {floor(x/k)}—is already capturing most of the sharing possible.

Now I'm analyzing the actual complexity of this DP approach. The set V of distinct values has size O(sqrt(x)), and computing each M(v) takes O(sqrt(v)) time. When I sum up the work across all values in V, I get O(x^{3/4}) total—which is actually worse than I initially thought. Let me recalculate to make sure I'm not making an error here.

The key insight is that we can optimize this by precomputing M(m) for all m up to some threshold y using a sieve, which costs O(y) time. For values larger than y, we still need the DP approach, but now we're only summing over O(x/y) terms instead of O(sqrt(x)). This reduces the DP cost to O(x/sqrt(y)). Balancing these two costs by setting y = x^{2/3} gives us the optimal O(x^{2/3}) complexity.

I'm wondering if we could push this further with another optimization layer—maybe precomputing M at even more points using a different technique like quadratic sieving.

But looking at the literature, algorithms like Deleglise-Rivat and Gourdon do add more optimization layers to reduce constants, though they don't fundamentally change that x^{2/3} exponent. To actually beat x^{2/3}, we'd need a completely different approach to the DP itself. The forward-substitution method inherently requires summing over all those large values, which seems unavoidable. I'm considering whether we could solve the underlying triangular system differently—maybe using matrix inversion with fast multiplication algorithms. If we could invert the triangular matrix in O(n^ω) time where n = O(sqrt(x)), we might get O(x^{ω/2}). But even with Strassen's algorithm where ω ≈ 2.81, this would give us O(x^{1.4}), which is actually worse than x^{2/3}.

The matrix does have special structure from those floor(x/k) values though—maybe that could be exploited somehow. But I'm going in circles here without concrete progress, so let me step back and check if any of the other approaches have yielded results.

Let me think about a genuinely novel direction. The convergence acceleration experiment confirmed that the zeta zero sum approach is stuck. Let me think about the problem from a completely different angle.

**Novel idea: Segment-wise computation of pi(x)**

The Meissel-Lehmer formula requires O(x^{2/3}) work. The bottleneck is computing special leaves. But what if we could decompose pi(x) into segments that can be computed independently?

pi(x) = pi(x) - pi(x/2) + pi(x/2) - pi(x/4) + ... 


This telescoping doesn't help directly. But I can express pi(x) as the count of primes in (x/2, x] plus pi(x/2), where the interval count could be computed via sieving. That's still O(x/2) though.

What if I apply inclusion-exclusion more strategically? I'm exploring a black-box model where I have oracle access to f(n) and need to compute sum_{n≤x} f(n). The complexity bounds here suggest sum d(n) is O(x^{1/3}).

For the Möbius function, the query complexity jumps to O(x^{2/3}), and I'm wondering if there's a matching lower bound and why the gap exists. The crucial insight is that d(n) = 1*1 is a self-convolution of smooth functions, while mu is the Dirichlet inverse of 1—it oscillates because 1 * mu = delta. So for computing D(x), I leverage this self-convolution structure with the hyperbola method, but for M(x), the inverse relationship creates a fundamentally different computational structure.

Now I'm realizing there's a potential approach using the identity sum_{d≤x} mu(d) * floor(x/d) = 1. If I rearrange this to isolate M(x), I get a recursive-like formula where computing M(x) depends on knowing the sum of mu(d) * floor(x/d) for d ≥ 2. The question is whether this sum can be computed efficiently by breaking it into ranges where mu is known at smaller arguments, which would give me a way to compute M(x) recursively.

The recursion M(x) = 1 - sum_{k=2}^{x} M(floor(x/k)) works because floor(x/k) only takes O(sqrt(x)) distinct values, making the total complexity O(x^{2/3}). But I'm wondering if I can optimize the inner sum further by grouping the k values into ranges where floor(x/k) is constant, then summing M at those distinct values with appropriate multiplicities instead of iterating through each k individually. ^{2/3})?

The dependency structure shows that M(v) needs M(floor(v/j)) for j=2,...,v, and these dependencies stay within the set V of values we're computing. Counting the total edges in this dependency graph gives roughly x^{3/4}, but the standard grouping approach still beats this with O(x^{2/3}) complexity.

The key insight is that most values in V are actually small—roughly half fall below x^{1/3}—so we can precompute those cheaply with a sieve in O(x^{2/3}) time. For the larger values, we use recursion on the O(x^{1/3}) remaining cases, where each takes O(sqrt(x)) work, but memoization and careful grouping bring the total down from O(x^{5/6}) to match the overall O(x^{2/3}) bound.

Now I'm exploring whether we can optimize further by tuning the sieve cutoff y = x^alpha. The sieve itself costs O(x^alpha), and there are O(x^{1-alpha}) large values to handle recursively, each requiring O(sqrt(x)) terms—though many are already cached from the sieve phase.

For each large value v = floor(x/k), the recursion involves O(sqrt(v)) distinct quotients, and the ones exceeding y need to be computed recursively. The total work across all large values turns out to be O(x/y * ln(x/y)), which suggests the complexity depends heavily on how we balance the sieve cutoff. I'm reconsidering the standard Lucy DP recurrence to get the exact complexity formula. (sqrt(x/k))

The sum: sum_{k=1}^{x/y} sqrt(x/k) = sqrt(x) * sum_{k=1}^{x/y} 1/sqrt(k) ~ sqrt(x) * 2*sqrt(x/y) = 2*x/sqrt(y)

So T(x, y) = O(y) + O(x/sqrt(y))

Minimizing: y = x/sqrt(y) => y^{3/2} = x => y = x^{2/3}

Giving T = O(x^{2/3}).

Now the question: can we use a more efficient sieve or a more efficient summation?

For the summation phase: the sum involves sqrt(x/k) for each k, which is the cost of computing M(floor(x/k)) from the recursion. If I could batch these computations or find a way to exploit the multiplicative structure of M...

The recursion M(v) = 1 - sum_{k=2}^{v} M(floor(v/k)) is really a Dirichlet summatory function inversion, and since M corresponds to 1/zeta(s) in the language of Dirichlet series, it has an Euler product structure. This means M is multiplicative, which might let me compute it more efficiently by factoring rather than summing. I'm trying to express the Möbius function as a product over primes dividing n, which reveals its multiplicative structure. For squarefree numbers, this becomes a product of -1 for each prime factor, giving me (-1)^k directly. But I'm realizing this algebraic perspective doesn't immediately lead to a faster computation method either. The P_k terms represent k-almost-prime counting functions, with P_1 dominating as the prime counting function itself and P_2 providing the main correction term. Computing all these terms takes O(x^{2/3}) total work.

I'm shifting gears to consider whether randomization could speed this up. If I sample random integers from [1, x] and test each for primality, the density of primes by the prime number theorem is roughly 1/ln(x), so achieving an error less than 1 would require O(x * ln(x)) samples by the central limit theorem—which is actually worse than the deterministic approach.

Even with importance sampling or cleverly biased distributions, I'd need to already know where the primes are, so randomization doesn't help for exact computation. This brings me back to the core question: is there an algebraic identity that could unlock faster computation? The divisor function D(x) = sum d(n) is tractable because d factors as a Dirichlet convolution, allowing the hyperbola method to reduce the problem to summing floor(x/a) over a range—I should explore whether similar structural properties exist for pi(x). 2, with y = x^{1/2}
- This works because we can split the lattice point counting into two halves

For M(x) = sum mu(n):
- mu = 1^{-1} (Dirichlet inverse)
- The "hyperbola method" analogue: M(x) * D_1(x) + ... = 1? No, the convolution is M * 1 = delta
- sum_{n≤x} (M * 1)(n) = sum_{d≤x} M(d) * floor(x/d) = 1
- This is a RECURSION for M, not a direct formula

The difference: for D(x), we have a BILINEAR identity: D(x) = sum_{ab≤x} 1, which splits into two independent indices. For M(x), the identity M * 1 = delta is an EQUATION INVOLVING M ITSELF.

Now I'm wondering if there's a similar bilinear decomposition for M(x) — something like M(x) = sum_{ab≤x} f(a) * g(b) where f and g are independently computable. I'm exploring whether the Möbius function has a useful factorization through Dirichlet series, but the relationships I'm checking (like 1/zeta(s) = zeta(2s)/zeta(s) times something) don't seem to lead anywhere productive.

The core issue is that M(x) is the summatory function of a Dirichlet inverse, and inverting a convolution is fundamentally harder than composing one — it's like how matrix multiplication is straightforward but inversion requires comparable or greater effort. Computing M(x) turns out to be equivalent in difficulty to computing pi(x) up to polylogarithmic factors, so any breakthrough on one would transfer to the other.

From a circuit complexity angle, if pi(x) could be computed with polynomial-size circuits of depth O(log^k x), that would place it in NC^k.  But for sequential polylogarithmic time, we'd need polylogarithmic-size circuits instead — a much stronger requirement. The only functions with polylog-size circuits are basic arithmetic operations on O(log x)-bit inputs, which live in TC^0. The problem with pi(x) is that despite having a small output (at most O(log x) bits), the actual computation required to determine it appears to be exponential in the input size, similar to computing 2^n or factorials.

Now I'm thinking about this more carefully: pi(x) takes an n-bit input and produces an n-bit output, so it's a function on n-bit strings. Comparing it to other functions, addition needs O(n) gates with constant depth, multiplication requires O(n log n) gates, and factorial needs Omega(n log n) gates since the output itself is that large. For pi(x), the output is O(n) bits, but I'm not sure yet what the lower bound on its circuit complexity actually is.

The key insight is that pi(x) is definitely not in AC^0 if we're restricted to bounded fan-in without threshold gates, but we have an upper bound from the Lagarias-Odlyzko method using 2^{n/2} * n^O(1) gates. The real question is whether pi(x) could have polynomial-size circuits—that would be extraordinary, suggesting there's some hidden computational shortcut despite the complexity of the explicit formula involving zeta zeros. But honestly, we have no idea either way; this is completely open.

Rather than searching for recent work right now, let me wait for the background agents to finish their analysis, then I can synthesize everything together. In the meantime, I want to run a concrete experiment on small cases. For n ≤ 20 (so x up to about a million), I can compute the truth table of pi(x) mod 2 and measure its circuit complexity through Fourier analysis, approximate degree, and sensitivity measures—these all give lower bounds on what circuits would need. If the Rademacher function of pi has high Fourier degree or other complexity properties, that would suggest pi(x) genuinely requires superpolynomial circuits.

Computing circuit complexity bounds...

content.thinking:
Let me think about a genuinely novel direction. The convergence acceleration experiment confirmed that the zero-sum barrier is solid for explicit formula methods. So let me think about something completely different.

**Novel idea: Can we compute pi(x) by computing something ELSE that determines it?**

Here's a thought: instead of computing pi(x) directly, what if we compute a related quantity that determines pi(x) but is easier to compute?

For example:
- The primorial p# (product of primes up to p) satisfies ln(p#) = theta(p) where theta is the Chebyshev function


- Euler's totient function and the Möbius function both encode information about primes, but they all circle back to the same fundamental complexity.

**Another idea: Dimensional reduction**

The Meissel-Lehmer formula works by breaking pi(x) into a sum over special pairs—a prime p paired with integers m ≤ x/p that have no small prime factors. The O(x^{2/3}) complexity emerges from optimizing how we balance this two-dimensional counting problem.

I'm wondering if a three-dimensional decomposition could push us down to O(x^{1/2}). The Deleglise-Rivat and Gourdon refinements already use multi-dimensional techniques, and LMO rearranged the computation to reduce memory overhead. But despite these improvements, the asymptotic complexity has stayed stuck at O(x^{2/3}/log^2 x) since 1985. The Lagarias-Odlyzko analytic method offers a different approach entirely.

The analytic method achieves O(x^{1/2+eps}) but with impractical constants. What if I could combine both approaches—use the combinatorial method to get a rough approximation of pi(x) with controlled error bounds, then use a small number of zeta zeros to pin down the exact value? The problem is that the combinatorial method produces exact results, not intermediate approximations I can refine. But the segmented Lucy_Hedgehog dynamic programming approach computes pi(x) by building a table of pi(floor(x/n)) for all n—maybe there's a way to exploit that structure.

Actually, those table entries are integer-valued step functions that don't interpolate cleanly. Let me shift perspective entirely. What if I treated this as a "black box" problem: if I could compute pi(x) mod p for various small primes p in polylogarithmic time, then the Chinese Remainder Theorem would reconstruct pi(x) efficiently. The challenge is that computing pi(x) mod p seems just as hard as computing pi(x) itself, unless there's some algebraic or number-theoretic identity linking pi(x) mod p to solutions of polynomial equations modulo p.

Thinking about pi(x) modulo small numbers more concretely: the parity of pi(x) is tied to the Liouville function and flips each time we encounter a prime, but that's circular—determining the exact flips requires already knowing where the primes are. I'm going in circles trying to find a shortcut that doesn't require knowing the primes themselves.

Let me reconsider the Lucy_Hedgehog sieve approach. If I compute everything modulo m, I do get pi(x) mod m, but the algorithm doesn't actually speed up—it's still O(x^{2/3}) operations, just with smaller intermediate values. The bottleneck is the sheer number of operations, not the size of the numbers themselves.

I need to think about this from a completely different angle.

What if I use character sums instead? Dirichlet character orthogonality lets me express the count of primes p ≤ x in a specific residue class a (mod q) as a weighted sum of character sums over primes. This connects to zeros of L-functions, but summing over all residues recovers pi(x)—except now I'd need zeros of every L-function, which is even more intractable than the Riemann Hypothesis.

There's also the question of Siegel zeros: if an L-function had a zero extremely close to s=1, it would severely skew the distribution of primes across residue classes.  But we have no evidence they exist, and even if they did, I'm not sure how to turn that into a practical algorithm.

Let me reconsider the Meissel-Lehmer structure itself. The special leaves are pairs (p, m) where p is a prime in a certain range and m is a number with specific divisibility properties. Each pair contributes a sign based on how many distinct prime factors m has. I'm trying to see if there's a pattern or shortcut in how these contributions combine.

The total number of special leaves is manageable—roughly O(x^{2/3}/log^2 x)—but computing each one requires looking up values in a precomputed table. I'm wondering if I could batch these lookups using FFT or number-theoretic transforms to speed things up. The challenge is that the summand depends on pi(x/(pm)), which is a table lookup that doesn't naturally decompose as a product, so FFT convolution doesn't immediately apply. I've also considered whether fast matrix multiplication could help if the special leaves could be organized as a matrix product, but again, the table lookups break the clean factorization I'd need.

I'm realizing I've been going in circles trying to optimize around the table lookups. Let me refocus on the Buchstab identity and the binary tree structure it creates—the recursion phi(x, a) = phi(x, a-1) - phi(x/p_a, a-1) has depth a and terminates early on most branches, giving O(x^{2/3}) total work. What if instead of sieving by primes in the standard order, I sieve by a different ordering that creates a more balanced tree?

Actually, wait—the order of sieving doesn't affect the final result due to inclusion-exclusion commutativity, but it does reshape the tree structure and thus the computational cost. Though the standard analysis of Buchstab recursion already optimizes the splitting point, so the O(x^{2/3}) bound is essentially tight for this approach.

Let me consider precomputing pi(n) for all values up to some threshold B and using those cached results to accelerate pi(x) for larger x. The Meissel-Lehmer algorithm already does this with its DP table storing pi values at key points, so I'd need to think about whether explicit precomputation adds anything beyond what's already there.

What if I precompute the first T zeta zeros to high precision and apply them directly in the explicit formula? That gives error O(x/T), which means I'd need T > x for error less than 1—so precomputation only helps if I'm willing to store O(x) zeros, which defeats the purpose. Even with convergence acceleration like Richardson extrapolation, I can reduce the constant factor but not change the asymptotic wall.

This whole exploration confirms that acceleration methods consistently hit the same zero-sum barrier—a meaningful negative result. The circuit complexity angle seems like the most promising direction to explore next.

While waiting, I want to dig deeper into the error structure itself. For a fixed number of zeros N, say 200, how does the error scale as x increases? If I can characterize error(x, N) ~ x^α · f(N), I can figure out the exchange rate—how many additional zeros I need for each order of magnitude jump in x.

content.text: Let me now investigate the **scaling relationship** between x and the number of zeros needed — this is the core question.

input.content:
"""
Zero Scaling Analysis: How does the number of zeros needed scale with x?
=========================================================================
Session 11 experiment.

For exact pi(x) via explicit formula, we need the truncation error < 0.5.
The error at N zeros is approximately |E(x,N)| ~ C * sqrt(x) * ln(N) / N.

This experiment measures the EXACT scaling empirically:
- For each x, find minimum N where round(S(N)) = pi(x) (for plain and accelerated)
- Fit N_min(x) to determine the scaling law
- Compare: N ~ sqrt(x)? N ~ x^{1/3}? N ~ log(x)^k?

This determines whether ANY constant-factor improvement from acceleration
translates to an asymptotic improvement.
"""

import mpmath
import math
import os
import sys
import time
import numpy as np

mpmath.mp.dps = 50

SCRIPT_DIR = os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__))
sys.path.insert(0, SCRIPT_DIR)
from riemann_explicit import R_function, R_at_rho, MOBIUS


def load_zeros(count):
    data_dir = os.path.join(SCRIPT_DIR, '..', '..', 'data')
    for try_count in [1000, 500, 300, 200]:
        fname = os.path.join(data_dir, f"zeta_zeros_{try_count}.txt")
        if os.path.exists(fname):
            with open(fname) as f:
                zeros = [line.strip() for line in f if line.strip()]
            if len(zeros) >= count:
                return zeros[:count]
    raise FileNotFoundError(f"Need {count} zeros")


def richardson_2pt(S1, S2, N1, N2, alpha=1):
    """2-point Richardson extrapolation."""
    n1a = N1 ** alpha
    n2a = N2 ** alpha
    return (n2a * S2 - n1a * S1) / (n2a - n1a)


def richardson_3pt(S1, S2, S3, N1, N2, N3, alpha=1):
    """3-point Richardson."""
    R12 = richardson_2pt(S1, S2, N1, N2, alpha)
    R23 = richardson_2pt(S2, S3, N2, N3, alpha)
    n2a = N2 ** (alpha + 1)
    n3a = N3 ** (alpha + 1)
    return (n3a * R23 - n2a * R12) / (n3a - n2a)


def find_min_N(exact, Rx, terms, method='plain', max_N=None):
    """Find minimum N where method gives exact rounding."""
    if max_N is None:
        max_N = len(terms)

    # Binary search for minimum N
    partial = 0.0
    partial_sums = []
    for t in terms[:max_N]:
        partial += t
        partial_sums.append(Rx - partial)

    if method == 'plain':
        for N in range(1, max_N + 1):
            if round(partial_sums[N-1]) == exact:
                # Check stability: verify next 5 values also round correctly
                stable = True
                for k in range(1, min(6, max_N - N + 1)):
                    if round(partial_sums[N-1+k]) != exact:
                        stable = False
                        break
                if stable:
                    return N
        return None

    elif method == 'richardson1':
        # Richardson order 1 with N//2 and N
        for N in range(10, max_N + 1, 2):
            N1 = N // 2
            N2 = N
            val = richardson_2pt(partial_sums[N1-1], partial_sums[N2-1], N1, N2)
            if round(val) == exact:
                # Check stability
                stable = True
                for k in range(2, min(12, max_N - N + 1), 2):
                    N1b = (N + k) // 2
                    N2b = N + k
                    if N2b <= max_N:
                        val2 = richardson_2pt(partial_sums[N1b-1], partial_sums[N2b-1], N1b, N2b)
                        if round(val2) != exact:
                            stable = False
                            break
                if stable:
                    return N
        return None

    elif method == 'richardson2':
        # Richardson order 2 with N//3, 2N//3, N
        for N in range(15, max_N + 1, 3):
            N1 = max(5, N // 3)
            N2 = max(N1 + 1, 2 * N // 3)
            N3 = N
            if N1 < N2 < N3 <= max_N:
                try:
                    val = richardson_3pt(
                        partial_sums[N1-1], partial_sums[N2-1], partial_sums[N3-1],
                        N1, N2, N3)
                    if round(val) == exact:
                        return N
                except:
                    pass
        return None

    return None


# Exact values of pi(x) for testing
EXACT_PI = {
    100: 25,
    200: 46,
    500: 95,
    1000: 168,
    2000: 303,
    5000: 669,
    10000: 1229,
    20000: 2262,
    50000: 5133,
    100000: 9592,
    200000: 17984,
    500000: 41538,
    1000000: 78498,
}


def main():
    print("Zero Scaling Analysis")
    print("=" * 80)
    print("Finding minimum N (# of zeta zeros) for exact pi(x) as x grows")
    print()

    max_zeros = 1000
    zeros_str = load_zeros(max_zeros)

    methods = ['plain', 'richardson1', 'richardson2']
    results = {m: [] for m in methods}

    for x_val in sorted(EXACT_PI.keys()):
        exact = EXACT_PI[x_val]
        sqrt_x = math.sqrt(x_val)

        print(f"\nx = {x_val:>10,}  pi(x) = {exact:>6d}  sqrt(x) = {sqrt_x:.1f}")

        # Compute terms
        t0 = time.time()
        x_mpf = mpmath.mpf(x_val)
        Rx = float(R_function(x_mpf))
        terms = []
        for gs in zeros_str[:max_zeros]:
            gamma = mpmath.mpf(gs)
            rho = mpmath.mpc(0.5, gamma)
            val = 2 * float(mpmath.re(R_at_rho(x_mpf, rho)))
            terms.append(val)
        elapsed = time.time() - t0

        for method in methods:
            min_N = find_min_N(exact, Rx, terms, method=method, max_N=max_zeros)
            if min_N is not None:
                ratio = min_N / sqrt_x
                results[method].append((x_val, min_N))
                print(f"  {method:15s}: N_min = {min_N:4d}  "
                      f"N/sqrt(x) = {ratio:.4f}  "
                      f"N/x^(1/3) = {min_N / x_val**(1/3):.4f}  "
                      f"N/ln(x)^2 = {min_N / math.log(x_val)**2:.4f}")
            else:
                print(f"  {method:15s}: NOT EXACT with {max_zeros} zeros")

    # Scaling analysis
    print("\n" + "=" * 80)
    print("SCALING ANALYSIS")
    print("=" * 80)

    for method in methods:
        data = results[method]
        if len(data) < 3:
            print(f"\n{method}: insufficient data for scaling analysis")
            continue

        xs = np.array([d[0] for d in data], dtype=float)
        Ns = np.array([d[1] for d in data], dtype=float)

        # Fit: log(N) = alpha * log(x) + C
        log_x = np.log(xs)
        log_N = np.log(Ns)

        # Use last 2/3 of data points for fit (more reliable at larger x)
        start = len(log_x) // 3
        coeffs = np.polyfit(log_x[start:], log_N[start:], 1)
        alpha = coeffs[0]
        C = coeffs[1]

        print(f"\n{method}:")
        print(f"  Fit: N_min ~ {math.exp(C):.3f} * x^{alpha:.4f}")
        print(f"  For x^{1/2} scaling, alpha should be 0.500")
        print(f"  For x^{1/3} scaling, alpha should be 0.333")
        print(f"  For polylog scaling, alpha should be ~0")
        print(f"  MEASURED alpha = {alpha:.4f}")

        # Extrapolate: at x = 10^100, how many zeros needed?
        N_10_100 = math.exp(C) * (1e100 ** alpha)
        print(f"  Extrapolated N for x=10^100: {N_10_100:.2e}")

        # Also fit N_min / sqrt(x) to see if ratio is constant
        ratios = Ns / np.sqrt(xs)
        print(f"  N/sqrt(x) ratios: {', '.join(f'{r:.4f}' for r in ratios[-5:])}")

    # Summary
    print("\n" + "=" * 80)
    print("SUMMARY")
    print("=" * 80)
    print("""
If alpha ≈ 0.5: Zeros needed scale as sqrt(x) — NO improvement possible via acceleration
If alpha < 0.5: Some acceleration is effective — worth investigating
If alpha ≈ 0: BREAKTHROUGH — polylog zeros suffice

For p(10^100), x ~ 10^102:
  sqrt(x) = 10^51 zeros → 10^51 zero computations → infeasible
  x^{1/3} = 10^34 zeros → still infeasible
  polylog(x) = 100^k zeros → FEASIBLE
""")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

    elif method == 'richardson1':
        # Richardson order 1 with N//2 and N
        for N in range(10, max_N + 1, 2):
            N1 = N // 2
            N2 = N
            val = richardson_2pt(partial_sums[N1-1], partial_sums[N2-1], N1, N2)
            if round(val) == exact:
                # Check stability
                stable = True
                for k in range(2, min(12, max_N - N + 1), 2):
                    N1b = (N + k) // 2
                    N2b = N + k
                    if N2b <= max_N:
                        val2 = richardson_2pt(partial_sums[N1b-1], partial_sums[N2b-1], N1b, N2b)
                        if round(val2) != exact:
                            stable = False
                            break
                if stable:
                    return N
        return None

    elif method == 'richardson2':
        # Richardson order 2 with N//3, 2N//3, N
        for N in range(15, max_N + 1, 3):
            N1 = max(5, N // 3)
            N2 = max(N1 + 1, 2 * N // 3)
            N3 = N
            if N1 < N2 < N3 <= max_N:
                try:
                    val = richardson_3pt(
                        partial_sums[N1-1], partial_sums[N2-1], partial_sums[N3-1],
                        N1, N2, N3)
                    if round(val) == exact:
                        return N
                except:
                    pass
        return None

    return None


# Exact values of pi(x) for testing
EXACT_PI = {
    100: 25,
    200: 46,
    500: 95,
    1000: 168,
    2000: 303,
    5000: 669,
    10000: 1229,
    20000: 2262,
    50000: 5133,
    100000: 9592,
    200000: 17984,
    500000: 41538,
    1000000: 78498,
}


def main():
    print("Zero Scaling Analysis")
    print("=" * 80)
    print("Finding minimum N (# of zeta zeros) for exact pi(x) as x grows")
    print()

    max_zeros = 1000
    zeros_str = load_zeros(max_zeros)

    methods = ['plain', 'richardson1', 'richardson2']
    results = {m: [] for m in methods}

    for x_val in sorted(EXACT_PI.keys()):
        exact = EXACT_PI[x_val]
        sqrt_x = math.sqrt(x_val)

        print(f"\nx = {x_val:>10,}  pi(x) = {exact:>6d}  sqrt(x) = {sqrt_x:.1f}")

        # Compute terms
        t0 = time.time()
        x_mpf = mpmath.mpf(x_val)
        Rx = float(R_function(x_mpf))
        terms = []
        for gs in zeros_str[:max_zeros]:
            gamma = mpmath.mpf(gs)
            rho = mpmath.mpc(0.5, gamma)
            val = 2 * float(mpmath.re(R_at_rho(x_mpf, rho)))
            terms.append(val)
        elapsed = time.time() - t0

        for method in methods:
            min_N = find_min_N(exact, Rx, terms, method=method, max_N=max_zeros)
            if min_N is not None:
                ratio = min_N / sqrt_x
                results[method].append((x_val, min_N))
                print(f"  {method:15s}: N_min = {min_N:4d}  "
                      f"N/sqrt(x) = {ratio:.4f}  "
                      f"N/x^(1/3) = {min_N / x_val**(1/3):.4f}  "
                      f"N/ln(x)^2 = {min_N / math.log(x_val)**2:.4f}")
            else:
                print(f"  {method:15s}: NOT EXACT with {max_zeros} zeros")

    # Scaling analysis
    print("\n" + "=" * 80)
    print("SCALING ANALYSIS")
    print("=" * 80)

    for method in methods:
        data = results[method]
        if len(data) < 3:
            print(f"\n{method}: insufficient data for scaling analysis")
            continue

        xs = np.array([d[0] for d in data], dtype=float)
        Ns = np.array([d[1] for d in data], dtype=float)

        # Fit: log(N) = alpha * log(x) + C
        log_x = np.log(xs)
        log_N = np.log(Ns)

        # Use last 2/3 of data points for fit (more reliable at larger x)
        start = len(log_x) // 3
        coeffs = np.polyfit(log_x[start:], log_N[start:], 1)
        alpha = coeffs[0]
        C = coeffs[1]

        print(f"\n{method}:")
        print(f"  Fit: N_min ~ {math.exp(C):.3f} * x^{alpha:.4f}")
        print(f"  For x^{1/2} scaling, alpha should be 0.500")
        print(f"  For x^{1/3} scaling, alpha should be 0.333")
        print(f"  For polylog scaling, alpha should be ~0")
        print(f"  MEASURED alpha = {alpha:.4f}")

        # Extrapolate: at x = 10^100, how many zeros needed?
        N_10_100 = math.exp(C) * (1e100 ** alpha)
        print(f"  Extrapolated N for x=10^100: {N_10_100:.2e}")

        # Also fit N_min / sqrt(x) to see if ratio is constant
        ratios = Ns / np.sqrt(xs)
        print(f"  N/sqrt(x) ratios: {', '.join(f'{r:.4f}' for r in ratios[-5:])}")

    # Summary
    print("\n" + "=" * 80)
    print("SUMMARY")
    print("=" * 80)
    print("""
If alpha ≈ 0.5: Zeros needed scale as sqrt(x) — NO improvement possible via acceleration
If alpha < 0.5: Some acceleration is effective — worth investigating
If alpha ≈ 0: BREAKTHROUGH — polylog zeros suffice

For p(10^100), x ~ 10^102:
  sqrt(x) = 10^51 zeros → 10^51 zero computations → infeasible
  x^{1/3} = 10^34 zeros → still infeasible
  polylog(x) = 100^k zeros → FEASIBLE
""")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

content.thinking:
This is very interesting! The measured scaling exponents are:

- **Plain**: alpha = 0.178 — N_min grows very slowly! But this is misleading because the data is noisy (N_min jumps around a lot) and at x=10^6 plain doesn't even work with 1000 zeros.
- **Richardson-1**: alpha = 0.365 — close to x^{1/3}. For x=10^100, needs 10^37 zeros. Still infeasible.
- **Richardson-2**: alpha = 0.251 — close to x^{1/4}! For x=10^100, needs 10^25 zeros. Still infeasible.


- The data is quite noisy due to the oscillatory nature of the error — sometimes a particular N happens to give a small error, sometimes not. But the key insight is that the measured exponents are all below 0.5, so acceleration is genuinely helping. Richardson-2 at alpha ≈ 0.25 is substantially better than the theoretical O(sqrt(x)) bound.

However, I'm working with a limited range here — only up to x = 10^6 with 1000 zeros. Theory says the exponent should approach 0.5 as x gets arbitrarily large, so the lower measured alpha might just reflect small-x behavior, lucky cancellations at these particular values, or a mismatch in how strict my stability criterion is. Even taking the optimistic alpha = 0.25 and extrapolating to x = 10^100 still gives 10^25 zeros needed — completely impractical.

So the real takeaway is that convergence acceleration only buys a constant factor improvement, not an asymptotic one. The fundamental scaling remains power-law in x, not polylogarithmic. This is actually a useful negative result worth documenting.

I'm noticing something curious though — Richardson-2 at x = 10^6 only requires N = 75, compared to over 1000 for the plain method. That N/sqrt(x) ratio of 0.075 is suspiciously good, but it's probably just a lucky accident where the error happens to be small at these specific node positions. Let me investigate the background agents and pull together what I've learned.

11	then pi(x) would be in TC^0 (threshold gates can count). But PRIMES in TC^0
12	is a major open problem. Can we find an alternative TC^0 path?

28	================================================================================
29	THEORETICAL ANALYSIS: pi(x) and TC^0
30	================================================================================
31	
32	1. WHAT IS TC^0?
33	================
34	
35	TC^0 = constant-depth, polynomial-size circuits with MAJORITY gates.
36	Equivalently: problems computable in O(1) parallel time with polynomially
37	many processors using threshold operations.
38	
39	Known to be in TC^0:
40	  - Addition, subtraction, multiplication (BFSS 1986)
41	  - Division (integer, with remainder) (Hesse-Allender-Barrington 2002)
42	  - Iterated multiplication (product of n numbers) (HAB 2002)
43	  - MAJORITY, exact threshold (by definition)
44	  - Sorting networks (AKS 1983, constant-depth version)
45	  - Powering: x^n mod m (Allender 1999, via iterated multiplication)
46	
47	NOT in TC^0 (or not known):
48	  - General factoring
49	  - PRIMES (unknown -- between AC^0[p] and P)
50	  - Discrete log
51	  - Any problem requiring superpolynomial circuits
52	
53	2. THE DIRECT PATH: PRIMES in TC^0 => pi(x) in TC^0
54	=====================================================
55	
56	If we could test primality in TC^0, then:
57	  pi(x) = sum_{k=2}^{x} PRIMES(k)
58	
59	This is just counting how many of x inputs satisfy a TC^0 predicate.
60	Since MAJORITY (and exact counting) is in TC^0, this gives pi(x) in TC^0.
61	
62	But PRIMES in TC^0 is WIDE OPEN. The best known:
63	  - PRIMES is in P (AKS 2002)
64	  - PRIMES is NOT in AC^0 (Allender-Saks-Shparlinski 2001)
65	  - PRIMES is NOT in AC^0[p] for any prime p (same paper)
66	  - Gap: AC^0[p] < ??? < P
67	
68	Note: AKS primality test has depth O(log^{O(1)} n), way above TC^0's O(1).
69	
70	3. THE LEGENDRE SIEVE PATH
71	===========================
72	
73	Legendre's formula: pi(x) = phi(x, pi(sqrt(x))) + pi(sqrt(x)) - 1
74	
75	where phi(x, a) = count of integers <= x not divisible by any of the
76	first a primes.
77	
78	Inclusion-exclusion:
79	  phi(x, a) = x - sum_{i<=a} floor(x/p_i) + sum_{i<j<=a} floor(x/(p_i*p_j)) - ...
80	
81	Analysis of TC^0 components:
82	  (a) floor(x/d) -- this IS in TC^0 (integer division, HAB 2002)
83	  (b) Each individual term is TC^0-computable
84	  (c) Summing polynomially many TC^0 terms: still TC^0
85	
86	THE PROBLEM: The inclusion-exclusion has 2^{pi(sqrt(x))} = 2^{O(sqrt(x)/ln(x))}
87	terms. This is SUPER-POLYNOMIAL in log(x) (the input size).
88	
89	Even for the Meissel-Lehmer optimization, the number of "special leaves" is
90	O(x^{2/3} / log^2(x)), which is still super-polynomial in log(x).
91	
92	CONCLUSION: The Legendre sieve does NOT give a TC^0 circuit because the

96	============================
97	
98	We have: pi(x) = sum_{n<=x} f(n) for various representations involving mu(n).

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
TC^0 Reduction Analysis for pi(x)
==================================

Investigation: Can pi(x) be reduced to functions known to be in TC^0?

This file is a combined theoretical analysis + computational experiment.

Key question: pi(x) = sum_{k=2}^{x} [k is prime]. If PRIMES were in TC^0,
then pi(x) would be in TC^0 (threshold gates can count). But PRIMES in TC^0
is a major open problem. Can we find an alternative TC^0 path?

Author: Claude (research agent), 2026-04-04
Project: prime-research, circuit complexity investigation
"""

import math
import time
from functools import lru_cache
from collections import defaultdict

# =============================================================================
# SECTION 1: THEORETICAL ANALYSIS
# =============================================================================

ANALYSIS = """
================================================================================
THEORETICAL ANALYSIS: pi(x) and TC^0
================================================================================

1. WHAT IS TC^0?
================

TC^0 = constant-depth, polynomial-size circuits with MAJORITY gates.
Equivalently: problems computable in O(1) parallel time with polynomially
many processors using threshold operations.

Known to be in TC^0:
  - Addition, subtraction, multiplication (BFSS 1986)
  - Division (integer, with remainder) (Hesse-Allender-Barrington 2002)
  - Iterated multiplication (product of n numbers) (HAB 2002)
  - MAJORITY, exact threshold (by definition)
  - Sorting networks (AKS 1983, constant-depth version)
  - Powering: x^n mod m (Allender 1999, via iterated multiplication)

NOT in TC^0 (or not known):
  - General factoring
  - PRIMES (unknown -- between AC^0[p] and P)
  - Discrete log
  - Any problem requiring superpolynomial circuits

2. THE DIRECT PATH: PRIMES in TC^0 => pi(x) in TC^0
=====================================================

If we could test primality in TC^0, then:
  pi(x) = sum_{k=2}^{x} PRIMES(k)

This is just counting how many of x inputs satisfy a TC^0 predicate.
Since MAJORITY (and exact counting) is in TC^0, this gives pi(x) in TC^0.

But PRIMES in TC^0 is WIDE OPEN. The best known:
  - PRIMES is in P (AKS 2002)
  - PRIMES is NOT in AC^0 (Allender-Saks-Shparlinski 2001)
  - PRIMES is NOT in AC^0[p] for any prime p (same paper)
  - Gap: AC^0[p] < ??? < P

Note: AKS primality test has depth O(log^{O(1)} n), way above TC^0's O(1).

3. THE LEGENDRE SIEVE PATH
===========================

Legendre's formula: pi(x) = phi(x, pi(sqrt(x))) + pi(sqrt(x)) - 1

where phi(x, a) = count of integers <= x not divisible by any of the
first a primes.

Inclusion-exclusion:
  phi(x, a) = x - sum_{i<=a} floor(x/p_i) + sum_{i<j<=a} floor(x/(p_i*p_j)) - ...

Analysis of TC^0 components:
  (a) floor(x/d) -- this IS in TC^0 (integer division, HAB 2002)
  (b) Each individual term is TC^0-computable
  (c) Summing polynomially many TC^0 terms: still TC^0

THE PROBLEM: The inclusion-exclusion has 2^{pi(sqrt(x))} = 2^{O(sqrt(x)/ln(x))}
terms. This is SUPER-POLYNOMIAL in log(x) (the input size).

Even for the Meissel-Lehmer optimization, the number of "special leaves" is
O(x^{2/3} / log^2(x)), which is still super-polynomial in log(x).

CONCLUSION: The Legendre sieve does NOT give a TC^0 circuit because the
number of terms exceeds any polynomial in the input size.

4. THE MOBIUS FUNCTION PATH
============================

We have: pi(x) = sum_{n<=x} f(n) for various representations involving mu(n).

Specifically, via Mobius inversion of the prime zeta function:

1	#!/usr/bin/env python3
2	"""
3	Mertens Function Speedup Investigation
4	=======================================
5	
6	Investigates whether M(x) = sum_{n<=x} mu(n) can be computed faster than O(x^{2/3}).
7	
8	Key questions:
9	1. Why does D(x) = sum d(n) admit O(x^{1/3}) via hyperbola, but M(x) is stuck at O(x^{2/3})?
10	2. Can the Helfgott-Thompson O(x^{3/5}) elementary approach be implemented/tested?
11	3. Is the Lucy_Hedgehog DP fundamentally limited at O(x^{2/3})?
12	
13	Background:
14	- D(x) = sum_{n<=x} d(n) = 2*sum_{k<=sqrt(x)} floor(x/k) - floor(sqrt(x))^2  [O(x^{1/3}) via Euler-Maclaurin]
15	- M(x) = sum_{n<=x} mu(n) uses identity: M(x) = 1 - sum_{n=2}^{x} M(floor(x/n))
16	- Best combinatorial: O(x^{2/3}) for both pi(x) and M(x) [Lucy_Hedgehog DP]
17	- Helfgott-Thompson (2021): O(x^{3/5} (log x)^{3/5+eps}) elementary for M(x)
18	- Analytic: O(x^{1/2+eps}) via Lagarias-Odlyzko / explicit formula with zeta zeros
19	
20	References:
21	- Helfgott & Thompson, "Summing mu(n): a faster elementary algorithm" (arXiv:2101.08773)
22	- Hiary, "Fast methods to compute the Riemann zeta function" (Annals 2011)
23	- gbroxey, "Summing Multiplicative Functions" (blog, 2023)
24	- Lucy_Hedgehog, Project Euler Problem 10 forum post
25	
26	Date: 2026-04-04
27	"""
28	
29	import math
30	import time
31	import sys
32	from collections import defaultdict
33	from functools import lru_cache
34	
35	# =============================================================================
36	# PART 1: Brute-force reference implementations (for verification)
37	# =============================================================================
38	
39	def mobius_sieve(n):
40	    """Compute mu(k) for k = 0..n using a linear sieve. O(n) time and space."""
41	    mu = [0] * (n + 1)
42	    mu[1] = 1
43	    is_prime = [True] * (n + 1)
44	    primes = []
45	    for i in range(2, n + 1):
46	        if is_prime[i]:
47	            primes.append(i)
48	            mu[i] = -1  # prime => mu = -1
49	        for p in primes:
50	            if i * p > n:
51	                break
52	            is_prime[i * p] = False
53	            if i % p == 0:
54	                mu[i * p] = 0  # p^2 divides i*p
55	                break
56	            else:
57	                mu[i * p] = -mu[i]
58	    return mu
59	
60	
61	def mertens_brute(x):
62	    """Compute M(x) = sum_{n<=x} mu(n) by brute force sieve. O(x) time."""
63	    mu = mobius_sieve(x)
64	    return sum(mu[1:x+1])
65	
66	
67	def divisor_sum_brute(x):
68	    """Compute D(x) = sum_{n<=x} d(n) by brute force. O(x log x) time."""
69	    d = [0] * (x + 1)
70	    for k in range(1, x + 1):
71	        for m in range(k, x + 1, k):
72	            d[m] += 1
73	    return sum(d[1:x+1])
74	
75	
76	# =============================================================================
77	# PART 2: D(x) via Dirichlet Hyperbola Method -- O(sqrt(x))
78	# =============================================================================
79	
80	def divisor_sum_hyperbola(x):
81	    """
82	    Compute D(x) = sum_{n<=x} d(n) using the Dirichlet hyperbola method.
83	
84	    Identity: D(x) = 2 * sum_{k=1}^{floor(sqrt(x))} floor(x/k) - floor(sqrt(x))^2
85	
86	    This works because d = 1 * 1 (Dirichlet convolution), so:
87	      sum_{n<=x} d(n) = sum_{n<=x} sum_{ab=n} 1
88	                      = #{(a,b) : ab <= x}  (lattice points under hyperbola)
89	
90	    The hyperbola method counts these by splitting at sqrt(x):
91	      = 2 * sum_{a<=sqrt(x)} floor(x/a) - floor(sqrt(x))^2
92	
93	    Complexity: O(sqrt(x)) -- just a single loop up to sqrt(x).
94	    """
95	    isqrt_x = int(math.isqrt(x))
96	    result = 0
97	    for k in range(1, isqrt_x + 1):
98	        result += x // k
99	    result = 2 * result - isqrt_x * isqrt_x
100	    return result

file.content:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
Mertens Function Speedup Investigation
=======================================

Investigates whether M(x) = sum_{n<=x} mu(n) can be computed faster than O(x^{2/3}).

Key questions:
1. Why does D(x) = sum d(n) admit O(x^{1/3}) via hyperbola, but M(x) is stuck at O(x^{2/3})?
2. Can the Helfgott-Thompson O(x^{3/5}) elementary approach be implemented/tested?
3. Is the Lucy_Hedgehog DP fundamentally limited at O(x^{2/3})?

Background:
- D(x) = sum_{n<=x} d(n) = 2*sum_{k<=sqrt(x)} floor(x/k) - floor(sqrt(x))^2  [O(x^{1/3}) via Euler-Maclaurin]
- M(x) = sum_{n<=x} mu(n) uses identity: M(x) = 1 - sum_{n=2}^{x} M(floor(x/n))
- Best combinatorial: O(x^{2/3}) for both pi(x) and M(x) [Lucy_Hedgehog DP]
- Helfgott-Thompson (2021): O(x^{3/5} (log x)^{3/5+eps}) elementary for M(x)
- Analytic: O(x^{1/2+eps}) via Lagarias-Odlyzko / explicit formula with zeta zeros

References:
- Helfgott & Thompson, "Summing mu(n): a faster elementary algorithm" (arXiv:2101.08773)
- Hiary, "Fast methods to compute the Riemann zeta function" (Annals 2011)
- gbroxey, "Summing Multiplicative Functions" (blog, 2023)
- Lucy_Hedgehog, Project Euler Problem 10 forum post

Date: 2026-04-04
"""

import math
import time
import sys
from collections import defaultdict
from functools import lru_cache

# =============================================================================
# PART 1: Brute-force reference implementations (for verification)
# =============================================================================

def mobius_sieve(n):
    """Compute mu(k) for k = 0..n using a linear sieve. O(n) time and space."""
    mu = [0] * (n + 1)
    mu[1] = 1
    is_prime = [True] * (n + 1)
    primes = []
    for i in range(2, n + 1):
        if is_prime[i]:
            primes.append(i)
            mu[i] = -1  # prime => mu = -1
        for p in primes:
            if i * p > n:
                break
            is_prime[i * p] = False
            if i % p == 0:
                mu[i * p] = 0  # p^2 divides i*p
                break
            else:
                mu[i * p] = -mu[i]
    return mu


def mertens_brute(x):
    """Compute M(x) = sum_{n<=x} mu(n) by brute force sieve. O(x) time."""
    mu = mobius_sieve(x)
    return sum(mu[1:x+1])


def divisor_sum_brute(x):
    """Compute D(x) = sum_{n<=x} d(n) by brute force. O(x log x) time."""
    d = [0] * (x + 1)
    for k in range(1, x + 1):
        for m in range(k, x + 1, k):
            d[m] += 1
    return sum(d[1:x+1])


# =============================================================================
# PART 2: D(x) via Dirichlet Hyperbola Method -- O(sqrt(x))
# =============================================================================

def divisor_sum_hyperbola(x):
    """
    Compute D(x) = sum_{n<=x} d(n) using the Dirichlet hyperbola method.

    Identity: D(x) = 2 * sum_{k=1}^{floor(sqrt(x))} floor(x/k) - floor(sqrt(x))^2

    This works because d = 1 * 1 (Dirichlet convolution), so:
      sum_{n<=x} d(n) = sum_{n<=x} sum_{ab=n} 1
                      = #{(a,b) : ab <= x}  (lattice points under hyperbola)

    The hyperbola method counts these by splitting at sqrt(x):
      = 2 * sum_{a<=sqrt(x)} floor(x/a) - floor(sqrt(x))^2

    Complexity: O(sqrt(x)) -- just a single loop up to sqrt(x).
    """
    isqrt_x = int(math.isqrt(x))
    result = 0
    for k in range(1, isqrt_x + 1):
        result += x // k
    result = 2 * result - isqrt_x * isqrt_x
    return result

content.text: Let me read the key findings from each agent's work.

content.content:
100	Specifically, via Mobius inversion of the prime zeta function:
101	  sum_{p<=x} 1 = sum_{k=1}^{log_2(x)} (mu(k)/k) * Pi(x^{1/k})
102	where Pi(x) = sum_{p^k <= x} 1/k (the prime power counting function).
103	
104	This is circular: Pi involves primes.
105	
106	Alternative: can we compute mu(n) in TC^0?
107	  mu(n) = 0 if n has a squared prime factor
108	  mu(n) = (-1)^k if n = p_1*...*p_k with distinct primes
109	
110	Computing mu(n) requires FACTORING n. Factoring is not known to be in TC^0.
111	(If factoring were in TC^0, then PRIMES would be in TC^0 trivially.)
112	
113	Even computing mu(n) mod 2 (= squarefree indicator) requires detecting
114	squared factors, which seems to require some form of factoring.
115	
116	CONCLUSION: Mobius path requires factoring, which is at least as hard as
117	PRIMES in TC^0 (and probably harder).
118	
119	5. THE PARITY QUESTION: pi(x) mod 2
120	=====================================
121	
122	Can we compute just the PARITY of pi(x)?
123	
124	pi(x) mod 2 is related to the Liouville function lambda(n) = (-1)^{Omega(n)}:
125	  sum_{n<=x} lambda(n) = count of n<=x with even number of prime factors
126	                        - count with odd number
127	
128	But pi(x) mod 2 is NOT directly equal to sum lambda(n).
129	
130	What IS true:
131	  (-1)^{pi(x)} = product_{p<=x} (-1) = (-1)^{pi(x)}
132	
133	  This is trivially circular.
134	
135	Better: pi(x) mod 2 = sum_{k=2}^{x} [k is prime] mod 2
136	
137	Even this requires testing primality of each number (or an equivalent).
138	
139	KEY INSIGHT: There is no known way to compute pi(x) mod 2 that avoids
140	the full difficulty of the problem. This suggests that even the least
141	significant bit of pi(x) is hard.
142	
143	Evidence: If pi(x) mod 2 were in TC^0, combined with the smooth part
144	R^{-1}(n) giving the top ~50% of bits, we'd only need the middle bits.
145	But no such "bit extraction" method is known.
146	
147	RELATED RESULT: The parity of the number of prime factors of n (computing
148	lambda(n)) can be done by factoring. But SUMMING lambda(n) for n <= x
149	(the summatory Liouville function L(x)) has the same O(x^{2/3}) complexity
150	as pi(x). This is strong evidence that parity doesn't help.
151	
152	6. SMOOTH NUMBER COUNTS AND TC^0
153	==================================
154	
155	Psi(x, y) = count of y-smooth numbers up to x.
156	
157	For FIXED y: Psi(x, y) counts integers <= x with all prime factors <= y.
158	The indicator "all prime factors of n are <= y" requires checking divisibility
159	by primes > y, which again needs PRIMES.
160	
161	For y = x^{1/u} with fixed u: the Dickman rho function gives
162	  Psi(x, x^{1/u}) ~ x * rho(u)
163	but this is an APPROXIMATION, not exact.
164	
165	Buchstab's identity: Psi(x, y) relates to Psi for different parameters,
166	but doesn't escape the fundamental need to identify primes.
167	
168	CONCLUSION: Smooth number counting doesn't provide a TC^0 path.
169	
170	7. POTENTIAL INDIRECT TC^0 PATH VIA ARITHMETIC
171	================================================
172	
173	The most promising angle: can pi(x) be expressed purely in terms of
174	arithmetic operations (which ARE in TC^0)?
175	
176	Consider: floor(x/n) for n = 1, ..., x takes only O(sqrt(x)) distinct
177	values. Can we compute pi(x) from these values alone?
178	
179	Lucy Hedgehog's algorithm (Lucy_Hedgehog on Project Euler, 2013) computes
180	pi(x) using only the values {floor(x/k) : k = 1, ..., x}. It's a DP
181	over O(sqrt(x)) states, each state being floor(x/k) for some k.
182	
183	The recurrence:
184	  S(v, p) = S(v, p-1) - [S(floor(v/p), p-1) - S(p-1, p-1)]
185	
186	where S(v, last_prime) counts numbers <= v surviving the sieve up to last_prime.
187	
188	The operations involved:
189	  - floor(x/k): TC^0 (integer division)
190	  - Subtraction: TC^0
191	  - Table lookup S(v, p-1): ???
192	
193	The PROBLEM is the depth of recursion. p ranges over primes up to sqrt(x),
194	so we iterate O(sqrt(x)/ln(x)) times. Each iteration depends on the previous.
195	This gives SEQUENTIAL depth O(sqrt(x)/ln(x)), not constant depth.
196	
197	8. THE FUNDAMENTAL OBSTACLE
198	=============================
199	
200	Every known approach to EXACT pi(x) has the same structure:
201	
202	  pi(x) = [smooth approximation] + [correction involving primes]
203	
204	The correction requires either:
205	  (a) Testing primality of individual numbers (PRIMES in TC^0: open)
206	  (b) Summing over known primes (circularity)
207	  (c) Zeta zero computation (O(x^{1/2}) terms minimum)
208	  (d) Sieve recursion of depth proportional to number of small primes
209	
210	None of these fit into constant-depth circuits of polynomial size.
211	
212	9. NEW OBSERVATION: TC^0 ORACLE SEPARATION
213	============================================
